coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-based fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor for thiacloprid detection
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yang
Cao, Nan
Gui, Wenying
Ma, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a test strip-based sensor was developed for thiacloprid quantitative detection based on PDA molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs). Thiacloprid is a new type of nicotine insecticide, which can block the normal neurotransmitter delivery process in insects. In the sensing system, N-GQDs were immersed into filter paper at first. Then, dopamine (DA) with thiacloprid can be self-polymerized on test strip surface to form the uniform PDA film. After removed thiacloprid template, the established poly dopamine (PDA) MIP can selectively recognize thiacloprid. As a result, captured thiacloprid can enhance the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs into the test strip. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs can be linearly related within a certain range of thiacloprid concentration. Under the optimum conditions, the proposed sensor for thiacloprid detection exhibited a linear ranging from 0.1 mg/L to 10 mg/L with a low detection limit of 0.03 mg/L. The N-GQDs based test strip-based sensor for thiaclopridis reported for the first time. The sensing system has high selectivity to thiacloprid and provides new opportunities in the pesticide detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GQDs
Fluorescence analysis
Thiacloprid
Molecularly imprinted polymer-based sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-GQDs and quantum yield (QY) measurements
Preparation of test strip based MIP sensor
The characterization of the prepared N-GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of N-GQDs. (B) Size distribution histograms of QDs in the optimal procedure.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-GQDs. (B) FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs.
Fluorescence spectra and the image under UV lamp (365 nm) of N-GQDs and N-GQDs/thiacloprid.
Fluorescence spectra and the image of test strip with thiacloprid under UV lamp.

(A) The effect of pH on PL intensity of N-GQDs. (B) The effect of pH on PL intensity of N-GQDs/thiacloprid. (C) The relationship between the plot of I/I0 value and the pH value. I and I0 referred to the PL intensity of the N-GQDs solutions in the presence and absence of thiacloprid, respectively.
PL intensity of filter paper after placing at placing at different time.
The standard curve of thiacloprid detection.
The effect of different pesticide on PL intensity of N-GQDs.
The process of the molecularly imprinted N-GQDs based fluorescence strip preparation.
The interference of coexisting substances in the determination of thiacloprid (4 × 10−3 mmol/L).
Results for detection of thiacloprid in the actual samples with MIP sensor and HPLC.
Comparison of the methods of the detection of thiacloprid.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-based fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor for thiacloprid detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a test strip-based sensor was developed for thiacloprid quantitative detection based on PDA molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs). Thiacloprid is a new type of nicotine insecticide, which can block the normal neurotransmitter delivery process in insects. In the sensing system, N-GQDs were immersed into filter paper at first. Then, dopamine (DA) with thiacloprid can be self-polymerized on test strip surface to form the uniform PDA film. After removed thiacloprid template, the established poly dopamine (PDA) MIP can selectively recognize thiacloprid. As a result, captured thiacloprid can enhance the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs into the test strip. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs can be linearly related within a certain range of thiacloprid concentration. Under the optimum conditions, the proposed sensor for thiacloprid detection exhibited a linear ranging from 0.1 mg/L to 10 mg/L with a low detection limit of 0.03 mg/L. The N-GQDs based test strip-based sensor for thiaclopridis reported for the first time. The sensing system has high selectivity to thiacloprid and provides new opportunities in the pesticide detection.

In the sensing system, N-GQDs with polydopamine MIP film were established on test strip surface to recognize thiacloprid selectively.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Thiacloprid is a novel member of the neonicotinoid family of systemic insecticides, which acts as agonists of the post-synaptic ligand operated ion channels of the nervous system [1,2]. Because thiacloprid can act on receptors of neurotransmitters, it can interfere with the normal nerve conduction of insects. As a result, it can cause the accumulation of a large amount of nerve conduction medium in the insect body and make the insects over-excited and ultimately die of the whole body spasm paralyzing. Therefore, in 2013, the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) enforced a 2 year Europe wide ban on the use of the three neonicotinoids, imidacloprid, thiacloprid and clothianidin [3]. Currently, the analytical methods of thiacloprid detection mainly depend on chromatography and MS analysis [4]. Although these detection methods are effective and have low detection limit, the common technologies are limited by complicated synthesis, long reaction time and high detection cost. Thus, a simple and rapid sensor for thiacloprid determination with high sensitivity and selectivity is needed urgently.

With the development of fluorescence sensor [5], more and more nanomaterials have been employed in the novel sensing systems. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a novel type of fluorescent material have attracted the notice of researchers due to their excellent properties of chemical inertness, ease of production, low cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility [6,7]. These excellent properties of GQDs make them have a wide range of applications of biological imaging, optical sensing, and organic photovoltaic devices [8]. However, the surfaces of GQDs can be modified hardly, which make the GQDs have low quantum yield (QY) and limit the potential application [9]. So the surface of GQDs we can be modified by doping other hetero-atoms, which not only maintain their intrinsic properties, but also improve the quantum yield of GQDs. For example, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have high quantum yield, superior fluorescent stability, stable photoluminescence characteristics and electrocatalytic activities. Therefore, they have more and more widely applications of sensors, fuel cells, and optoelectronics and so on. Xue et al. have used electrochemical method to prepare N-GQDs with fluorescence and electrochemical catalytic activity [10]. N-GQDs-based electrochemiluminescence sensors have also used to detect small molecules, organic compounds and proteins [11].

In this paper, we used test strip-based fluorescence sensor to detect thiacloprid with molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) and N-GQDs. Molecular imprinting technique is a technology to identify target molecule specifically by making molecularly imprinted polymer. Recently, MIP is applied in more and more fields. Compared to traditional methods, MIP has higher mechanical and chemical stability, high specific recognition ability, and low cost. The interaction of polymers and the template molecule mainly includes covalent bonding and non-covalent bonding [12]. Scheme 1 described the thiacloprid sensing process of test strip-based fluorescence sensor. DA with thiacloprid was self-polymerized on test strip surface to form the uniform PDA MIP film in this work. DA is a neurotransmitter. The main role is to help the cells send pulses of chemical substances [13]. DA can be self-polymerization in aqueous solution conditions, and absorbed on the metal, glass, wood and a series of solid materials on the surface [14]. So, the PDA films formed on the test stripcan work as a biocompatible matrix for the molecules immobilization and reduce nonspecific absorption. Based on the enhanced photoluminescence characteristics of N-GQDs with thiacloprid and high selectivity of MIP, a novel sensor for thiacloprid was presented in this work. It is the first report that N-GQD based thiophanate sensor by utilizing MIP and using test strip as the solid matrix.

All chemicals and reagents were analytical grade and used directly without further purification. Thiacloprid, citric acid (CA), ammonia, DA, Tris, sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were obtained from Beijing Dinggguo Chemicals Co. (Beijing, China). All reagents were prepared using ultrapure water with a resistivity of greater than 18 MΩ cm−1. The water samples were collected from the underground water in Jilin University. All experiments were carried out at room temperature.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra measurements were performed on a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired on a UV-1200 UV–vis Spectrophotometer with a 1 cm quart cell. Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was conducted on a Thermo Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrometer using KBr pellets. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained with a Hitachi electron microscope operating at 200 kV. TEM samples were prepared by dropping the silica microspheres onto carbon-coated copper grids and allowing the excess solvent to evaporate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-GQDs and quantum yield (QY) measurements
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were synthesized by carbonization of CA with ammonia according to the previous reports [15]. 2 g of citric acid and 0.3 mL of ammonia were transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 210 °C for 1 h. The resultant dark brown mixture (2 g) was dissolved to 10 mL with ultrapure water. Subsequently, pH of N-GQDs dispersion was tuned to 7.0 with adding NaOH solution. The supernatant was collected by removing the large dots through centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. Then the obtained liquid was diluted to 20 mL with water acquiring N-GQDs stock solution with concentration of 0.1 g/mL. Finally, the as-prepared N-GQDs were stored at 4 °C for further characterization and use.
The QY of the resultant N-GQDs was calculated according to the follow equation [20]:

Y
u
=
Y
s
I
u
/
I
s
A
s
/
A
u
n
u
2
/
n
s
2
where Y represents the quantum yield, I represents the measured integrated emission intensity, A represents the absorbance intensity, n is the refractive index of the solvent, u represents the N-GQDs, and s represents the quinine sulfate standard sample (QY = 0.54).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of test strip based MIP sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we diluted the solution of N-GQDs with a concentration of 0.1 g/L. The filter paper was immersed in the diluted N-GQDs solution for 10 min. And then the test strip was dried with absorbed by N-GQDs. We prepared Tris buffer solution with a concentration of 10 mmol/L, and adjusted its pH value by 8.5. The concentration of thiacloprid and DA were 50 mg/L (0.2 mol/L) and 2 g/L respectively. The dried test strip absorbed by N-GQDs was immersed in thiacloprid and DA solution for 4 h. Then, unpolymerizated DA was washed and removed from the test strip. The modified PDA MIP was washed with ultrapure water and immersed in 5% wt% acetic acid/SDS solution for an hour to remove thiacloprid template. In the detection process, the excitation wavelength of the N-GQDs was 360 nm and the emission wavelength was 460 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of the prepared N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 showed the TEM image of the as-prepared N-GQDs in this work. N-GQDs were ca. 5 nm and the quantum yield of the N-GQDs was 43%. As is shown in Fig. 2A, there is a maximum absorption peak at 340 nm in the UV spectrum. This is because of the n-π* transition of the C˭O covalent bond. The FT-IR spectrum shows two peaks at 1178 cm−1 and 1231 cm−1, respectively. This was due to the stretching vibration of C-OH. And the absorption of stretching vibration of C˭O is at 1706 cm−1. And the absorption peaks at 3439, 3062 cm−1is due to the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H. Peak at 1376 cm−1 is because of the C-NH in-plane bending vibration. Fig. 3 shows the PL spectrums of N-GQDs solution in the presence and absence of thiacloprid. It is obviously that the PL intensity of N-GQDs enhanced largely with the addition of thiacloprid. The inset in Fig. 3 displays the photos of N-GQDs solution under the UV lamp. The brightness fluorescence of the N-GQDs with thiacloprid has increased significantly. It is due to the C≡N covalent bonds and unsaturated heterocylic rings containing S and N in thiacloprid. Thiacloprid has reduced the surface disordered oxygen-containing groups of N-GQDs, resulting in a PL enhancement of N-GQDs. With the increase of the degree of N-GQDs reduction, the oxygen groups on the surface of N-GQDs reduced gradually. As a result, the occurrence of n-π* transition can be reduced, which causes the corresponding number of graphite phenolic defects increase. The radiation transition of the electron-hole pairs in the topological defects of the graphite phenols can enhance the blue fluorescence emission.

The PL spectrums of N-GQD based test strip, N-GQD/MIP test strip and N-GQD/MIP/thiacloprid test strip are shown in Fig. 4. There is no significant change of the PL intensity in the N-GQD/MIP test strip. But the PL intensity of N-GQD/MIP/thiacloprid test strip has enhanced significantly. The PL increase on the test strip indicates that the sensor has good selectivity. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4, N-GQD/MIP test strip and N-GQD/MIP/thiacloprid test strip are irradiated with UV lamp (365 nm) respectively. The change of PL intensity of the sensing system on the test stripcan be distinguished by naked eyes.

To achieve of optimal conditions, the absorption time and the preparation elution time of the test strip based MIP sensor are investigated. After the preparation of PDA film coating thiacloprid on the surface of N-GQDs test strip, the embedded thiacloprid in the test strip need to be eluted. The elute time is set as 0 h, 0.5 h, 1 h, 1.5 h, 2 h, 3 h respectively. When the elute time reaches to 1 h, the PL intensity of the sensor is the closest to the original PL of N-GQDs on the test strip without thiacloprid. To test the optimal pH value of thiaclopriddetection, Tris buffer solutions of different pH are added into the sensing system. The concentration of thiacloprid was 2.5 mg/L (10 mmol/L). Fig. 5a and b were the PL spectrums of N-GQDs and N-GQDs with thiacloprid under different pH conditions, respectively. I and I0 referred to the PL intensity of the N-GQDs solutions in the presence and absence of thiacloprid, respectively. As is shown in Fig. 5c, the value of I/I0 increases gradually in the range of pH 5.0–7.0 and decreases in the range of pH 7.0–10.0. The PL intensity enhancement ratio of N-GQDs was the highest at pH 7.0. It indicated that the reaction of N-GQDs with thiacloprid is pH-dependent. Regarding the hydrolysis stability, thiacloprid is unstable and easy to decompose under alkaline conditions at pH 9–12. Although thiacloprid is stable under acid conditions, N-GQDs showed poor photoluminescence properties at pH < 7. So, the optimal pH value is 7.0 and the results are consistent with previous report [24]. The immersed time with thiacloprid of the prepared test strip based MIP sensor is discussed in Fig. 6. The immersed time is set as 0 min, 5 min, 10 min, 30 min, 60 min, 90 min, and 180 min, respectively. The PL intensity of the sensor keeps stable at 30 min. Therefore, the optimal absorption time is chosen as 30 min.

The PL intensity of N-GQDs increased with the concentration of thiacloprid from 0.1 to 10 mg/L, and with the concentration of thiacloprid in the range of 0.1–10 mg/L, the PL intensity of the sensor and the concentration of thiacloprid showed a good linear relationship. The linear regression equation was Y = 41.08x + 146.80 (Y and x represented the PL intensity and the concentration of thiacloprid respectively). And the coefficient of correlation was R2 = 0.911. The detection limit was 0.03 mg/L and the limit of quantification was 0.1 mg/L. European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) regulated the maximum residue of thiacloprid in vegetables at 0.05 and our linear range covered this very well Fig. 7. In the Table 3, we compared the detection results of our work with previous reports [16–19]. Our MIP sensor has lower LOD than UV–vis spectrometry method. Although the complex, expensive and time-consuming HPLC and MS methods had ideal LOD, the proposed MIP sensor provided a simple and rapid detection for thiacloprid with high selectivity. To detect the selectivity of N-GQDs to thiacloprid, we determine the PL intensity of N-GQDs with the addition of other pesticides such as tebuconazole, imidacloprid, thiamethoxam and chlorothalon. We added 10 μL N-GQDs to the solution and other pesticide standards. The concentration of the solution was 10 mmol/L. The result of this experiment is shown in Fig. 8. In order to further test the sensing ability of the sensor for thiacloprid detection, various interfering components are systematically added to the thiacloprid solution at1mg/L (4 × 10−3 mmol/L). We allow the determination error within 5% to detect the following substances on the determination of thiacloprid.

It can be seen from Table 1 that the interference of thiamethoxam, imidacloprid in the concentration of 10 times of the concentration of thiacloprid,clothianidin in the concentration of 100 times of the concentration of thiacloprid, fipronil in the concentration of 1000 times of the concentration of thiacloprid on the measurement method could be ignored. (ΔI = I − I0). In order to assess the reliability and accuracy of the sensor, HPLC was also employed to analyze the water samples for comparison. The results obtained by the standard addition method were shown in the
Table 2. The recovery of the PL sensor detecting thiacloprid was between 101% and 110% with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 4.52%. The results were consistent with those of HPLC method, which proved the feasibility of the sensor.

A novel test strip based MIP sensor has been developed for thiacloprid detection. The established PDA MIP can specifically capture and selectively recognize the target thiacloprid. And the captured thiacloprid can increase the PL intensity of N-GQDs effectively. 0.1–4 mg/L thiacloprid can be detected on the test strip. This was the first time that N-GQDs/MIP based sensor for thiacloprid detection is reported. Most importantly, this sensor provided a rapid, portable, and reliable analysis method for pesticide detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21075050 and 21005029) and Youth Science Fund of Jilin Province (20140520081JH).
